attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the gastroenterology clinic with a recent diagnosis of Crohn's disease confined to the terminal ileum. She has experienced a 12-month history of recurrent right iliac fossa pain, with each episode lasting about three days and associated with abdominal distension and constipation before spontaneous relief of symptoms. Her last episode was 3 months ago.

She has type 2 diabetes, which is well controlled with dietary modifications. She does not take any regular medications apart from the oral contraceptive pill. She has no other significant medical history.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her BMI is 32 kg/m².

Investigations reveal a faecal calprotectin of 550 µg/g (<50), haemoglobin of 100 g/L (115-165) and a mean corpuscular volume of 75 fL (80-100). Ileocolonoscopy shows a 5 cm segment of active inflammatory Crohn's disease in the terminal ileum, through which the scope passes easily. Her MR enterography shows a 5 cm segment of active ileal inflammation without evidence of fibrosis, and no pre-stenotic dilatation or internal fistula formation.

She is provided with iron supplements.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management of her Crohn's disease?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has Crohn's disease affecting the terminal ileum with evidence of active inflammation (elevated faecal calprotectin) and complications (iron-deficiency anaemia) despite being currently asymptomatic. The natural history of Crohn's disease is one of progressive bowel damage if left untreated, making early intervention crucial.

Azathioprine is an appropriate first-line immunomodulator for this patient. While therapeutic guidelines typically recommend corticosteroids as first-line therapy to induce remission in active Crohn's disease, this patient's type 2 diabetes makes steroids a less favourable option due to their adverse effects on glycaemic control. Current evidence supports early immunomodulation in Crohn's disease to prevent disease progression and complications, rather than waiting for repeated flares.

Specialist inflammatory bowel disease physicians would typically implement aggressive step-up therapy, with 3-6 months of azathioprine followed by quick escalation to biologic therapy if needed. A repeat MR enterography or intestinal ultrasound in 6 months is recommended to ensure resolution of inflammation. Intestinal ultrasound is a relatively new but excellent modality for patients with limited ileal disease, taking only 20 minutes with no preparation and avoiding the severe diarrhoea often caused by MR enterography preparation with sorbitol.

Prednisolone would typically be first-line therapy for inducing remission in active Crohn's disease. However, given her symptoms were last experienced 3 months ago, it would be appropriate to start with azathioprine and then add a corticosteroid if required. In addition, systemic corticosteroids may significantly worsen glycaemic control in this patient. Budesonide could be considered as an alternative for treating ileal disease, as it has fewer systemic effects than prednisolone.

5-aminosalicylic acid preparations have not been shown to be effective in the treatment of small bowel Crohn's disease, particularly ileal disease. While they are effective for ulcerative colitis and possibly colonic Crohn's disease, they have limited efficacy in ileal disease.

Stricturoplasty is a surgical procedure used to treat fibrostenotic Crohn's disease with narrowing of the intestinal lumen. In this case, there is no evidence of stricturing as the endoscope passed easily through the affected segment and MR enterography confirms the absence of fibrosis or pre-stenotic dilatation.

Surgical resection of the affected bowel segment is generally reserved for patients with complications such as strictures, fistulae, or abscesses, or for those who have failed medical therapy. This patient has inflammatory rather than stricturing disease and has not yet tried medical therapy.
learningPoints: Corticosteroids are first-line therapy to induce remission in active Crohn's disease but may be contraindicated in patients with diabetes.
Early immunomodulation in Crohn's disease prevents disease progression and complications.
Active inflammation in Crohn's disease requires treatment even during symptom-free periods to prevent complications.
references: Lichtenstein GR, Loftus EV, Isaacs KL, et al. ACG Clinical Guideline: Management of Crohn’s Disease in Adults. Am J Gastroenterol 2018; 113:481-517.
Cushing K, Higgins PDR. Management of Crohn Disease: A Review. JAMA. 2021;325(1):69-80. doi:10.1001/jama.2020.18936
Therapeutic Guidelines. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited. Available from: https://www.tg.org.au
Australian Medicines Handbook (online). Adelaide: Australian Medicines Handbook Pty Ltd. Available from: https://amhonline.amh.net.au/
